lines: * @param locale             locale used by the break iterator (the {@code boundary_scanner_locale} option).
* @param orderByScore       when {@code true} fragments are returned by descending score instead of document order
+ * @param analyzerName       {@code analyzer} override from WITH, also shown in plan descriptions; {@code null} selects
* @param maxAnalyzedOffset  per-field analysis bound; a negative value means "use the default index setting" in the
* @param analyzer           analyzer used to build the per-row memory index and configure the unified highlighter.
@@ -111,6 +116,7 @@ public HighlightConfig withExecutionContext(Analyzer analyzer, Query query, List
+                + "no_match_size=0, word_boundary=false, locale=, order_by_score=false, analyzer=null, max_analyzed_offset=-1, fields=1]"
@@ -404,6 +405,7 @@ private static HighlightConfig config(String queryText, int fragments, int fragm
# Basic single-field highlight, default <em> tags, single short sentence.
ROW content = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
@@ -14,7 +14,7 @@ The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
# Custom prefix renames the generated highlight column from highlight_<field> to <prefix><field>.
ROW content = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
@@ -28,7 +28,7 @@ The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
# If prefix + column collides with an existing column, HIGHLIGHT will replace the column.
ROW content = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
@@ -43,7 +43,7 @@ The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
# Empty prefix overwrites the source column name instead of creating highlight_<field>.
ROW content = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
@@ -57,7 +57,7 @@ The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
# Empty prefix applies the same overwrite behavior to each ON field.
ROW title = "Return of the King", body = "Tolkien wrote the epic saga."
| HIGHLIGHT prefix = "" "king tolkien" ON title, body
@@ -71,7 +71,7 @@ Return of the King | Tolkien wrote the epic saga.
# Non-empty prefix keeps the source column and adds <prefix><field>.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -85,7 +85,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron. | The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# Prefix and WITH options can be used together.
ROW content = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
@@ -99,7 +99,7 @@ The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
ROW content = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
@@ -114,7 +114,7 @@ match | The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
ROW content = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
@@ -128,7 +128,7 @@ The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
# Multiple query terms (OR'd) wrap independently within a single sentence.
ROW content = "Sauron forged the One Ring in the fires of Mount Doom."
# Matching is case-insensitive; original casing preserved.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -156,7 +156,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# HTML encoder escapes markup and special characters while still wrapping matches.
ROW content = "Use bold tags & special chars with the Ring."
@@ -170,7 +170,7 @@ Use <b>bold</b> tags & special chars with the Ring
# No match in the field -> null (default no_match_size = 0).
ROW content = "A plain text with no matching terms."
# no_match_size > 0 makes a non-matching field fall back to its leading text instead of null.
ROW content = "Gardens and flowers bloom in spring."
@@ -198,7 +198,7 @@ Gardens and flowers bloom in spring.
# Query text that analyzes to no terms still behaves as a no-match query.
# Multiple ON fields each get their own highlight_<field> column.
ROW title = "Return of the King", body = "Tolkien wrote the epic saga."
@@ -226,7 +226,7 @@ Return of the King | Tolkien wrote the epic saga.
# A field that the query does not match yields null while the other is populated.
ROW title = "Return of the King", body = "An ordinary description."
# Multi-valued field: every value is highlighted, order preserved.
# boundaries (SplittingBreakIterator on the MV separator); this is an intentional divergence from Query DSL, where a
# fragment can span values when no sentence boundary separates them and they fit within fragment_size.
# number_of_fragments is Lucene maxPassages: best-N by score in document order (not first-N).
# A single short sentence stays one fragment regardless of number_of_fragments.
# number_of_fragments = 0 disables fragmentation: the whole value is returned as one fragment, matches still wrapped.
ROW content = "Elasticsearch is fast. Elasticsearch is scalable. Elasticsearch is open."
# HIGHLIGHT over fields loaded from an index, after a full-text WHERE filter.
# Sorting on a highlight_* column forces HIGHLIGHT to run before the final TopN, so it is
# pushed down to the data nodes; this exercises HighlightExec serialization end-to-end.
# Empty prefix overwrite is preserved when HIGHLIGHT is pushed to data nodes by SORT.
# HIGHLIGHT runs after the final SORT ... LIMIT (TopN), like Query DSL's post-fetch highlight.
# The original field is preserved alongside the appended highlight_<field> column.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -403,7 +403,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron. | The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# fragment_size bounds each sentence-based fragment; a large value lets both sentences share one fragment.
ROW content = "Elasticsearch is fast. Elasticsearch is scalable."
# A small fragment_size keeps each sentence in its own fragment, so the two matching sentences become two values.
ROW content = "Elasticsearch is fast. Elasticsearch is scalable."
# boundary_scanner=sentence (the default) breaks fragments on sentences, so the match brings back the whole sentence.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron. Frodo carried it to Mount Doom."
@@ -445,7 +445,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron. Frodo carried it to Mount Doom.
# boundary_scanner=word breaks on word boundaries, so the fragment is scoped to the matched word.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron. Frodo carried it to Mount Doom."
# boundary_scanner_locale picks the break-iterator Locale; en-US breaks like the default here, so output is unchanged.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -473,7 +473,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# order=none (the default) keeps fragments in document order regardless of how many matches each one has.
ROW content = ["fast search", "fast and fast results"]
# order=score reorders fragments by descending score: the second value has two matches, so it comes first.
ROW content = ["fast search", "fast and fast results"]
# max_analyzed_offset caps how far analysis runs; "One" ends at offset 7, so it is still matched under a cap of 10.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -515,7 +515,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# A match past the max_analyzed_offset cap is never seen: "Sauron" is beyond offset 10, so the row is null.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
# boundary_chars, boundary_max_scan and phrase_limit are FastVectorHighlighter-only, so they are accepted but no-ops.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -543,7 +543,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# Unquoted adjacent terms wrap independently; use a quoted phrase for a single span.
ROW content = "I love New York City in the spring."
@@ -557,7 +557,7 @@ I love New York City in the spring.
# A quoted phrase matches the exact word sequence and weight-matches wraps it in a single span.
ROW content = "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog."
@@ -571,7 +571,7 @@ The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
# order=score with a number_of_fragments cap keeps the highest-scoring fragment: the last value wins despite its order.
ROW content = ["one fast result", "a slow result", "fast and fast wins"]
# no_match_size bounds the leading text returned for a non-matching field, trimmed to the first sentence here.
ROW content = "Gardens and flowers bloom in spring. Bees hum across the meadow. Rain falls in autumn."
@@ -599,7 +599,7 @@ Gardens and flowers bloom in spring
# In a multi-valued field only matching values are returned; the single match here comes back as a single value.
# An empty query analyzes to no terms and, with the default no_match_size = 0, yields null.
# Column pruning: with two ON fields, KEEP can drop one highlight_<field> column without breaking the block layout.
ROW title = "Return of the King", body = "Tolkien wrote the epic saga."
# Column pruning can also drop the original ON columns while keeping only the requested highlight column.
ROW title = "Return of the King", body = "Tolkien wrote the epic saga."
# Column pruning over fields loaded from an index: two ON fields are highlighted but only highlight_title is kept.
# RENAME a single highlight column: the renamed column carries the highlighted value, the other keeps its name.
ROW title = "Return of the King", body = "Tolkien wrote the epic saga."
@@ -688,7 +688,7 @@ Return of the King | Tolkien wrote the epic saga.
# RENAME both highlight columns: each one is renamed independently and keeps its own highlighted value.
ROW title = "Return of the King", body = "Tolkien wrote the epic saga."
@@ -703,7 +703,7 @@ Return of the King | Tolkien wrote the epic saga.
# DROP one highlight column: dropping highlight_title leaves highlight_body with its correct value.
ROW title = "Return of the King", body = "Tolkien wrote the epic saga."
# DROP the original ON columns but keep both highlight columns, leaving only the two highlighted columns.
ROW title = "Return of the King", body = "Tolkien wrote the epic saga."
@@ -731,7 +731,7 @@ Return of the King | Tolkien wrote the epic saga.
# RENAME the original ON column before HIGHLIGHT: it highlights the renamed column and names its output accordingly.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -746,7 +746,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# Boolean AND requires every term to be present; both are highlighted when the row matches.
ROW content = "The fox and the dog play."
# Boolean AND with a missing term does not match the row, so the highlight is null.
ROW content = "The fox jumps over the lazy cat."
# A prohibited term that is present suppresses the whole match (Query DSL boolean semantics), yielding null.
ROW content = "The fox and the dog play."
# A trailing wildcard is a prefix query and matches every token starting with the prefix.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -802,7 +802,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# Leading wildcards are allowed (ES query_string default), so "*auron" matches "Sauron".
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -817,7 +817,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# A pattern with several wildcards matches every token fitting it: "*a*n*" matches any token with an "a" before an
# "n", so both Gandalf and Sauron are wrapped while "faced" (an "a" but no following "n") is not.
# A bare fuzzy term uses AUTO fuzziness: "Sauro~" is one edit from "Sauron".
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -845,7 +845,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# A regex term between slashes matches "Sauron".
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
@@ -860,7 +860,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron.
# A regexp is matched case-sensitively against the analyzed (lowercased) term, mirroring Query DSL query_string.
# The uppercase pattern "/M(ount|t)/" never matches the indexed term "mount", so the row is null.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron in the fires of Mount Doom."
# The lowercase pattern "/m(ount|t)/" matches the indexed term "mount"; the original casing is kept when wrapped.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron in the fires of Mount Doom."
@@ -888,7 +888,7 @@ The One Ring was forged by Sauron in the fires of Mount Doom.
# A term qualified on a different field parses but never highlights (require_field_match: true), yielding null.
ROW content = "The One Ring was forged by Sauron."
# Grouping with parentheses combines a disjunction and a required term; both matched terms are wrapped.
ROW content = "The fox jumps over the lazy dog."
| HIGHLIGHT "(fox OR cat) AND jumps" ON content
# A conjunction spanning two ON fields matches the row and highlights each targeted field.
ROW title = "The quick fox", body = "A loyal dog"
# A MATCH(...) function query targets its field and highlights it exactly like the equivalent literal.
# Sorting by the highlight column pushes this MATCH query to the data nodes.
# The ':' match operator behaves like MATCH as a HIGHLIGHT query.
* Support for PromQL {@code histogram_quantile()} over classic histograms with {@code le} buckets.
